design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore world, defeat elemental bosses to collect four swords, combine into Crystalis to defeat final antagonist. Secondary loop: engage enemies with appropriate elemental weakness, gain experience to increase stats, acquire new spells and armor.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Elemental sword matchups against enemies and environmental puzzles; resource management of MP for spells; level gating on bosses (must reach minimum level or sword type deals no damage)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Post-apocalyptic hero awakening from cryogenic sleep with no memory, gradually learning magical combat and gathering legendary elemental weapons to prevent totalitarian regime from weaponizing an ancient Tower
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game enforces elemental puzzle-solving: each sword (Wind, Fire, Water, Thunder) is mandatory for traversal and combat. Barriers require specific elemental blasts; ice bridges require Water sword; certain enemies are immune to non-matching elements. This creates a gated, authored experience rather than player-chosen approach—you must use the correct tool for each situation.

difficulty.checkpointing: Manual save system via menu; no automatic checkpoints. Death returns player to last manually saved location.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Level-gating mechanic: bosses and certain enemies cannot be damaged until player reaches specific minimum level with correct sword type, forcing engagement with grinding. Elemental immunities prevent bypassing puzzle design. Hit detection windows (odd/even frame-based) reward frame-perfect play but are not required for casual progression.

progression.upgrade_system: Experience-based leveling increases health, defense, and attack strength. Equipment system: acquire multiple suits of armor and shields that stack defensive bonuses. Spell acquisition tied to sage encounters (Zebu, Tornel, Asina, Kensu); spells provide utility (Telepathy for sage advice, healing, etc.).
progression.unlock_mechanism: Elemental swords gated by dungeon completion and boss defeats. Environmental traversal unlocked by obtaining correct sword (Wind for pushing objects, Water for ice bridges, Fire/Thunder for barriers). Plot-gated: certain areas locked until NPCs are consulted or kidnapping sequence triggers. Telepathy spell unlocks ability to receive sage guidance remotely.
replayability_hooks: Multiple elemental swords with distinct mechanics encourage replaying encounters differently
Price-swapping glitch (move cursor then select to pay wrong NPC's price) and damage-boosting suggest speedrun community adoption
Four recurring 'Dragonia's Finest Four' bosses with distinct elemental weaknesses create pattern-learning replay value

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling: post-apocalyptic medieval setting shows nuclear war regression (magic adoption after 1997 thermonuclear catastrophe)
NPC dialogue in towns exposes lore piecemeal; Telepathy spell creates remote dialogue mechanic with four sages
Protagonist amnesia framing (protagonist cannot recall name, was cryogenically frozen pre-war) creates mystery narrative hook
Boss character arcs: 'Dragonia's Finest Four' recur multiple times, suggesting personality and loyalty to Draygon
Floating Tower visual icon represents both goal and thematic oppression (Draygon seeks to weaponize it for conquest)

boss_design.philosophy: Elemental matchup system: each of four recurring bosses (Kelbesque, Sabera, Mado, Karmine) has distinct elemental weakness requiring specific sword. Level-gating ensures boss encounters occur when player is sufficiently powered. Boss encounters teach elemental mechanics gradually—early encounters are survivable with grinding, later encounters demand proper element selection and dodging. Final boss Draygon has true form reveal after false-self defeat, adding narrative twist to mechanical challenge.
notable_bosses: General Kelbesque (elemental weakness documented, encountered twice)
Karmine the Wizard (encountered in Goa fortress endgame, skeleton enemies in lair; paralyzed skeletons do not collapse, allowing efficient stab kills)
Emperor Draygon (ultimate antagonist, false self then true dragon-form final phase)

faction_indicators: Draygonian Empire controls world via Tower; occupants govern via weapons systems
Four sages (Zebu, Tornel, Asina, Kensu) represent resistance/wisdom faction guiding protagonist
Mesia as female scientist accomplice from protagonist's era—loyalty tie to pre-war civilization values
danger_signals: Draygon revived forbidden science combined with magic, destabilizing post-war magic-only society
Tower weaponry threatens world governance; Draygon's Tower entry attempt is core conflict
Mutated creatures populate wasteland, suggesting ongoing environmental threat from nuclear aftermath
environmental_storytelling: Towns like Portoa serve as medieval-styled settlements in post-apocalyptic frame—survivors downgraded to primitive existence
Elemental barriers and environmental puzzles reflect sword-magic fusion as primary technology
Cryogenic chamber (Mezame Shrine) preserves pre-war artifacts and freezing technology as ancient mystery
Mt. Sabre, Evil Island, Goa fortress as distinct biomes with elemental theming (ice climbing, fire/water puzzle zones)
